label: dalziel::physostigma_venenosum
text: Physostigma venenosum — Calabar Bean, Efik: Esère, Calabar Nigeria. Dalziel (1937). Used as ordeal poison in judicial trials and by healers for eye conditions ('clouding of vision' = glaucoma). Daniel (1846) documented; Christison (1855) pharmacological study; Fraser (1863) isolated physostigmine (eserine). First glaucoma treatment: Laqueur (1876). Active compound: physostigmine — reversible acetylcholinesterase inhibitor. Foundation of cholinergic pharmacology. Modern descendants: neostigmine, pyridostigmine (myasthenia gravis, nerve agent antidote).

label: dalziel::garcinia_kola
text: Garcinia kola — Bitter Kola. Yoruba: Orogbo. Igbo: Akiilu. Dalziel (1937). Seeds chewed for cough, chest complaints, liver disease, male fertility. Pharmacology: kolaviron (biflavonoid) — hepatoprotective, anti-inflammatory. Clinical evidence: Annals of Thoracic Medicine (2009) clinical study showing lung function improvement. HIV: anti-HIV activity studied in vitro. Traditional use for respiratory conditions validated by bronchodilator activity of flavonoid components.

label: dalziel::khaya_antimalarial
text: Khaya senegalensis — African Mahogany, Dry Zone. Hausa: Madaci. Distribution: Sahel belt. Dalziel (1937). Bark decoction for fever, malaria, intestinal complaints. Among the most widely used medicinal trees in the West African Sahel — documented across dozens of ethnic groups with consistent malaria indication. Pharmacology: khivorin, senegalin (limonoids) — documented antimalarial activity in vitro and rodent models. Multiple Burkina Faso, Mali, Senegal studies showing significant anti-Plasmodium falciparum activity. One of the most promising underdeveloped antimalarial plants in Africa.

label: dalziel::securidaca_salicylate
text: Securidaca longipedunculata — Violet Tree. Hausa: Sanya. Distribution: dry savanna, pan-African. Dalziel (1937). Root bark used for chest complaints, rheumatism, snakebite. Documented as toxic — controlled medicine restricted to healers. Pharmacology: methyl salicylate 60-80% of essential oil — highest documented natural salicylate concentration in any known plant. Methyl salicylate converts to salicylic acid in the body (same mechanism as aspirin). This makes Securidaca the most potent natural aspirin-equivalent plant documented in any tradition in this archive. Convergence: salicylate plants for pain documented in every tradition; this adds the highest-concentration example.

label: dalziel::combretum_kinkeliba
text: Combretum micranthum — Kinkeliba. Mandé: Kinkeliba. Wolof: Kinkéliba. Sahel belt, Senegal to Nigeria. Dalziel (1937). Leaves decocted as daily health tea for jaundice, liver disease, fever, diabetes. Most widely consumed herbal tea in the Sahel — drunk daily by millions. Commercially sold throughout francophone West Africa as standardised herbal tea sachets. Pharmacology: vitexin, orientin (flavone glycosides) — hepatoprotective in animal models; choleretic (stimulates bile flow — consistent with jaundice use); hypoglycemic activity. The most commercially developed traditional West African medicine.

label: dalziel::morinda_antimalarial
text: Morinda lucida — Brimstone Tree, Yoruba: Oruwo, Igbo: Nkacha. Forest zone Nigeria, Ghana. Dalziel (1937). Bark decoction for malaria, jaundice, typhoid — most widely prescribed antimalarial plant by Yoruba traditional healers. Pharmacology: damnacanthal, morindone, rubiadin (anthraquinones) — documented antiplasmodial activity in vitro at IC50 values competitive with chloroquine. Multiple Nigerian studies confirming activity. Internal convergence: consistent documentation across different Yoruba healer lineages and geographic areas strengthens the antimalarial claim.

label: dalziel::african_healer_role
text: Traditional Healers in West Africa — Dalziel (1937) and subsequent ethnobotany. Healer types: herbalists (plant medicines for internal conditions); diviners (spirit consultation for diagnosis, then herbal treatment); bone-setters (trauma, fractures); birth attendants (obstetric specialists); snakebite specialists. The integration of diagnosis through divination and treatment through plant medicine in a single healer role parallels Aztec h-men, Tibetan physician-priests, and Ayurvedic Brahmin physicians — a universal structure across widely separated traditions. Dalziel notes: 'Knowledge of plant properties among Africans is extensive and practical — typically 5-10 years apprenticeship, memorizing hundreds of preparations, indications, contraindications, and accompanying prayers.'

label: dalziel::neem_adopted
text: Azadirachta indica — Neem Tree. Hausa: Dogon yaro. Yoruba: Igi oba. Introduced to West Africa pre-colonially from South Asia; fully integrated into traditional medicine. West African uses: leaves decocted for malaria fever; bark for jaundice; seed oil for skin disease and insect repellent; leaves burned to repel mosquitoes. Independent identification of many applications beyond those brought with the plant. Pharmacology: azadirachtin (insecticidal), nimbidin, quercetin. Malaria clinical trials: neem leaf decoction tested in multiple West African clinical trials; consistent antiplasmodial biological activity despite mixed clinical results. Represents a case where a plant medicine was adopted from another tradition and independently extended — evidence of active pharmacological experimentation rather than passive receipt of knowledge.

label: dalziel::parkia_fermented
text: Parkia biglobosa — African Locust Bean, Dawadawa. Hausa: Dawa-dawa. Yoruba: Igba. Savanna belt. Dalziel (1937). Seeds fermented to produce dawadawa (major food condiment); bark for dysentery; leaves for wound healing, fever; roots for hypertension and eye conditions. Dawadawa fermentation produces compounds with documented antimicrobial activity against S. aureus and E. coli — validating condiment use as simultaneous food preservation and health maintenance. Exemplifies the food-medicine continuum widespread in West African tradition, where the same plant serves as staple food, condiment, and medicine.

label: dalziel::prostratin_hiv
text: Homalanthus nutans (now classified separately from initial West African identification, but relevant to West African tradition context) — HIV research connection. Prostratin discovery context: Paul Alan Cox and ethnobotanical work in Samoa documented traditional healer's use of specific plant for 'yellow fever-like illness' (possibly hepatitis). The prostratin compound (phorbol ester analog) isolated from this plant shows HIV latency reversal — forcing latent HIV out of hiding, potentially enabling immune system clearance. West African context: multiple West African plants with related phorbol esters (Euphorbia, Croton species) used by traditional healers for viral illnesses — the convergent identification of phorbol ester-containing plants for viral illness across Pacific and African traditions is a significant under-investigated signal. Status: prostratin in Phase 1 trials for HIV treatment. Represents the 'medicines discovered through traditional medicine' pathway this archive aims to systematise.
